name: BFS Flood Fill (explicit queue)
summary: Scan for unvisited land; each time, flood its whole connected component breadth-first with a FIFO queue and count it once.
visualizationIntent: init: draw the grid with water cells dimmed and land cells idle, an empty state overlay, an empty queue, count=0. loop: scan cells row by row looking for unvisited land. check: test whether the scanned cell is land and not yet seen. update: on finding new land, tick the island count, enqueue the seed cell and mark it frontier. move: dequeue the front cell (current, vermillion), mark it done/visited, and enqueue its unvisited land neighbors as frontier (amber) — the island fills as an expanding wavefront. return: show the final island count.

lineExplanations.3: Record how many rows the grid has.
lineExplanations.4: Record how many columns each row has.
lineExplanations.5: Start an empty 'state' grid that will remember which cells we've reached (0 = unseen, 1 = waiting in the queue, 2 = done).
lineExplanations.6: Go through each row so we can give it a matching row of zeros in the state grid.
lineExplanations.7: Add a fresh row of zeros — one slot per column — so every cell starts unseen.
lineExplanations.8: Start the island counter at zero.
lineExplanations.9: Placeholder for the row of the cell we're currently flooding; -1 means 'none yet'.
lineExplanations.10: Placeholder for the column of the cell we're currently flooding; -1 means 'none yet'.
lineExplanations.11: The four moves that reach a cell's up, down, left, and right neighbors (diagonals don't count).
lineExplanations.12: Scan every row of the grid, top to bottom, looking for land to start a new island.
lineExplanations.13: Within the current row, scan every column left to right.
lineExplanations.14: Have we found a new island? True only when this cell is land ('1') AND we haven't reached it before.
lineExplanations.15: Found untouched land — this is a brand-new island, so add one to the count.
lineExplanations.16: Create an empty queue to hold the cells of this island still waiting to be explored.
lineExplanations.17: Enqueue the starting cell of the island at the back.
lineExplanations.18: Mark the starting cell as seen (1) so we never enqueue it twice.
lineExplanations.19: head marks the front of the queue — the index of the next cell to process.
lineExplanations.20: Keep flooding while the front hasn't caught up to the back (queue not empty).
lineExplanations.21: Look at the cell currently at the front of the queue.
lineExplanations.22: Advance the front marker so the next loop takes the following cell (this is the dequeue).
lineExplanations.25: Mark this cell fully done (2) — it now belongs to the current island.
lineExplanations.26: Try each of the four directions from this cell.
lineExplanations.27: Compute the neighbor's row in that direction.
lineExplanations.28: Compute the neighbor's column in that direction.
lineExplanations.29: Is the neighbor a valid, un-reached piece of land? (inside the grid, is '1', and still unseen).
lineExplanations.30: Mark that land neighbor as seen (1) so it isn't queued again.
lineExplanations.31: Enqueue the neighbor at the back to explore it later — the wavefront expands.
lineExplanations.32: This island is fully flooded — clear the current-row marker so the cursor disappears while we scan on.
lineExplanations.34: All cells scanned — return the total number of islands found.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: is the class wrapper LeetCode expects your solution to live in.
syntaxExplanations.2: def numIslands(self, grid): defines the method; grid is the 2D list of '1'/'0' characters.
syntaxExplanations.3: len(grid) counts the outer list's items — the number of rows.
syntaxExplanations.4: grid[0] is the first row; len(...) of it gives the number of columns.
syntaxExplanations.5: [] creates an empty list; we'll append one sub-list per row to it.
syntaxExplanations.6: for r in range(rows): loops r over 0, 1, ... up to rows-1.
syntaxExplanations.7: [0] * cols builds a list of that many zeros; .append(...) adds it as a new row.
syntaxExplanations.8: count = 0 initializes an integer variable to zero.
syntaxExplanations.9: cur_r = -1 sets a starting sentinel value; -1 is not a valid grid index.
syntaxExplanations.10: cur_c = -1 does the same for the column.
syntaxExplanations.11: A list of [row-delta, col-delta] pairs for the 4 orthogonal directions.
syntaxExplanations.12: for sr in range(rows): loops the scan row index over every row.
syntaxExplanations.13: for sc in range(cols): loops the scan column index over every column.
syntaxExplanations.14: and requires BOTH conditions to be true; grid[sr][sc] indexes row then column; '1' is the land character.
syntaxExplanations.15: count = count + 1 increases the counter by one.
syntaxExplanations.16: [] makes an empty list that we'll use as a FIFO queue (append at the back, read from the front).
syntaxExplanations.17: append([sr, sc]) adds a [row, col] pair at the back of the queue.
syntaxExplanations.18: state[sr][sc] = 1 stores 1 at that cell to mark it seen.
syntaxExplanations.19: head = 0 starts the front pointer at the first queued cell.
syntaxExplanations.20: while head < len(queue): runs while the front hasn't passed the last enqueued cell.
syntaxExplanations.21: queue[head] reads (without removing) the cell at the front.
syntaxExplanations.22: head = head + 1 moves the front forward — effectively removing that cell.
syntaxExplanations.23: cell[0] reads the first element of the [row, col] pair.
syntaxExplanations.24: cell[1] reads the second element — the column.
syntaxExplanations.25: state[cur_r][cur_c] = 2 marks this cell as fully processed.
syntaxExplanations.26: for d in dirs: loops d over each [row-delta, col-delta] direction.
syntaxExplanations.27: cur_r + d[0] adds the direction's row offset to get the neighbor's row.
syntaxExplanations.28: cur_c + d[1] adds the column offset to get the neighbor's column.
syntaxExplanations.29: Chained 'and' checks bounds (0 <= nr < rows, 0 <= nc < cols), that it's land, and that it's unseen.
syntaxExplanations.30: state[nr][nc] = 1 marks the neighbor seen before it goes on the queue.
syntaxExplanations.31: append([nr, nc]) adds the neighbor at the back to explore later.
syntaxExplanations.32: cur_r = -1 resets the sentinel so no cell looks 'current' during the next scan.
syntaxExplanations.34: return count hands back the final answer.
